claims.text: Silencing an opinion harms more people than the speaker who is silenced.
If a suppressed opinion turns out to be true, everyone who was denied the chance to hear it has been robbed of the opportunity to exchange error for truth.
If a suppressed opinion is false, the loss caused by silencing it is subtler but still real.
When an opposing error is silenced, a true view loses the clearer and livelier impression that comes from colliding with that error.
Those who silence discussion always assume their own infallibility.
The assumption of infallibility made by those who silence discussion is rarely stated openly, but it is implicit in the act of silencing.
To forbid a question from being argued is to decide that question for others without allowing them to hear the other side.
Past ages have executed people for saying things that later ages regarded as obviously correct.
The people who executed others for their opinions were not villains but respectable men applying the received wisdom of their time.
Even a true belief degrades into prejudice if it is held without ever being tested.
A person who cannot state the strongest case against their own position does not really know that position.
Most people who hold correct opinions hold them in a half-dead way, without really knowing them.
Most people who hold correct opinions can recite the conclusion but cannot reconstruct the argument for it.
When their belief is seriously challenged, most people who hold correct opinions defend it badly or abandon it.
A rare devil's advocate is worth more to a community than a hundred conforming believers.
If no dissenter exists in a community, a dissenter ought to be invented.
The discipline of answering objections is the only thing that keeps a true belief alive.
A society that succeeds in extinguishing all its heretics also extinguishes its ability to explain why its orthodoxy deserved to win.

theses.text: Suppressing an opinion is always a mistake.
Silencing an opinion harms the whole community rather than only the speaker, whether the opinion turns out to be true or false.
A true belief that is never tested against dissent degrades into prejudice, so free discussion is necessary to keep true beliefs alive and rationally held.
To silence discussion is to assume one's own infallibility.
